item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're 8 now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if you're aware or care, but MTF Rho-9 is already taken.
http://www.scp-wiki.net/task-forces

item.title: Re: Most curious.
item.content:encoded:
Maybe pair of memos, an argument between O5 and Ethics Comittee or O5 and Analytics where one brings up how the containment procedures wouldn't actually stop a citizen from walking out if he became determined to do so (something like "there aren't enough SRAs on the planet to stop them if 5 of these guys decided to demanifest a quarter mile hunk of the border wall")

Then O5 says "we know, but the cleanup would almost certainly result in a Torn Veil so we made a treaty, we keep everyone out, they keep everyone in, and the world keeps spinning"

item.title: Re: Most curious.
item.content:encoded: I don't think i saw that in the article. You might consider clarifying that with a memo from 05. The only thing that kept me from giving a +1 is the immersion break I took when my brain noticed the fridge horror that was a town of (effectively) uncontained Type Greens

item.title: Re: Most curious.
item.content:encoded: I don't like to write the Foundation as an organization that destroys dangerous anomalies. If I took that route, this would have been a very different article. There is an in-universe explanation though for why the Foundation doesn't take more active measures though, which is that Greenfield is too powerful to risk confronting directly.

item.title: Re: Most curious.
item.content:encoded:
See, here's the issue with that

The Foundation has a well documented history of taking swift, often extreme responses to even the most benign sapient threats to baseline reality.

If the foundation discovered a SCiP that says "anyone who steps into this region becomes a contagious reality bender" it's much more likely they would stage a natural disaster (read, nuke the joint) and set up a 10 kilometer kill-on-sight perimeter around the active zone.

Heck, this might even be a 001 proposal. Imagine one person decides to go globetrotting, not knowing that every one of his dreams and the dreams of every one he runs into is generating an anomaly.

Imagine a girl who grows up in this town gets curious about the secret agents patrolling the town limits and decides "the whole world should know about this". That's a broken masquerade right there.

I'm not saying your SCiP is bad, I think you are just underestimating the destructive potential of your SCiP

item.title: Re: Most curious.
item.content:encoded:
They don't have super powers, just a slight distortion of reality's material. New walls, new stairs, etc.

The ones in separate realities are ghosts to others. Dunno what's going on with that Mayor. He was the catalyst, and so appears in all worlds?

Could use that speech as a litmus test, it might determine how people react to the town?

item.title: Most curious.
item.content:encoded:
So… it's a town and everyone who enters it becomes a (roughly) class 2 reality bender…

At first it sounded cliche. Series 4 has way too many worldscrews as it stands. Which brings me to a fairly significant plothole.

So… why hasn't the Foundation noto nuked this place from orbit? I can't remember the SCiP I'm referencing but the gist of this town is "if one child in this town dreams abut being in charge of the Death Star we are all super screwed"

Just saying. Seems like an oversight

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: they all have president names

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had no idea that one of the hardest parts of writing this article would be choosing colors. Go for it, and thanks for helping make this piece more accessible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it just my display, or is there very little color difference between the "gold" text and the black/dark gray default text? I had to look pretty hard to tell which was which. Rejekyll, would you mind if I adjusted the colors for greater contrast? (I'll make sure to keep it legible to red/green-colorblind people, and will try to do so for blue/yellow as well.)

(edit) Here's the proposed change. According to some pretty credible sources (1, 2), this should be easily distinguishable to people with regular vision and those with all three types of color blindness.

Approximately 40% of subjects report the addition of orange text in the speech, 15% report the addition of the light blue text, and 25% report the addition of dark blue text. The remaining ten percent report a mixture of two or three.

+ And here's the transcript, recolored.
– hide block

Greetings, people of Ridgefort. I am here tonight to deliver a message of unity and strength. This message comes deeply from my heart, and I hope you all heed my words. The time for quarreling and division is behind us. We now stand in the precipice of greatness. Who will join us? And who will perish?

A new era is beginning. A wave is sweeping across our town, a surge of optimism and pride that will crush all who oppose it with a power/that will carry us into an age the likes of which we have not seen before. These next few years will define who we are, what our town is. The land we leave to our children. The world we inhabit. What will we leave for the future of Ridgefort? Will we be defined by our surroundings, or rise above them and crush the pitiful sheep around us/like an eagle soaring through the sky/as we ought to, as children of God?

My fellow Ridgefortians, we need not hide who we are from pathetic humans/from those who do not understand our way of life. Our destiny is too great to live shackled to this place, this country, this world. This universe. Friends, I offer you only what all men desire: freedom and divinity. Same as our forefathers left the Old World for America, so must we too leave this world for a new one. And we must bring our dirty/beautiful town with us, for it is our home.

Pull, pull with all your strength, my people. Ridgefort will rise to the heavens and leave this disaster of a world in the ashes/behind/behind. Those of you who do not aspire to greatness, I weep for you. For you hold us back, and will die/that is a great tragedy/will die mercifully. Our town already bears the scars of violence. Those among us who wish to see more like that infernal Warner and his cronies/like Glen Warner must not prevail. Even now, the streets bulge and shudder under the weight of our indecision. This great tug of war must end. We must act with conviction to transform Ridgefort into the nightmare/city on a hill/paradise it was meant to be.

Some of our brothers and sisters have already begun the arduous process of this amazing migration. They are not long for this world, my friends, for they are soon to inhabit a new one. A better one. They are ghosts; ghoulish specters with no faces and no humanity./Their countenances are translucent, for most of their souls reside in a new plane. Soon we all shall. Even the enemies of our people, our neighbors who chain us to this forsaken universe, will find glory in the new world we will create for ourselves. Or they will die painfully. None shall oppose our abhorrent/holy/glorious endeavor, or they will die. Believe me, they will. They all will.

Merry Christmas and a happy New Year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The links to prior assignments bug me, personally. A note about "members have previous experience with anomalous urban locations" would be fine, but the links feel too involved, like the author's pawning a chunk of the work off on other authors/on the readers. And I open enough tabs on my own; I don't need the author giving me six more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. They gave information that explained what the anomaly actually is, and I think they would complete it much better if you added them right before or after the final speech.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you should add back in the interviews. They were powerful and would strengthen this article quite a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This piece originally relied much more on the backstory, but I stripped the article down in order to post it in time for the contest. The nature of the town is elucidated in the interviews, some of which I included in the previous discussion page (but not in the article). At some point I may add a supplement with interviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The overwhelming majority of feedback was to change the log format. However, it's still in the page history if you or anyone else wants to read the original presentation.

item.title: Updates
item.content:encoded: Alright motherfuckers, I stitched the logs together so it's easier to read. I also made a few small cosmetic changes here and there. Give it a reread if you took issue with the previous format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me, or did the MTF members shoot each other due to the SCP's anomalous effects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be quite nice to have some sort of combined log available as part of the SCP, or perhaps something that lets you link between them all, because I found it frustrating to see only one side of the conversations at a time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this enough to upvote. Personally the horror of inconsistent reality is highly effective. I think the split logs work as a literary device here, but fail as a plausible format the Foundation would use.

One question: What does "Alt-Gock" mean? And is this supposed to be the MTF's nickname (in which case should it have quotation marks)? Also noting that Alpha-9 is already "Last Hope" from Resurrection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked some of it, 'eh' on the rest. I'm gonna ignore the split log issue, since I went ahead and read the google doc. Judged without that factor, though, this doesn't draw me in enough. That may be a simple issue of personal preference — I love exploration logs and reality warping stuff, but I want interesting and unexpected imagery and events beyond what we have here.

What we have here is pretty okay beyond that, though. For other readers, the mundanity might be a plus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Title had me laughing in conjunction with the second log. Interesting angle you went in.

I like that each of the individuals had their own history of assignments and backgrounds. Nice detail there.

At this point, you've heard more than enough about those logs. They hurt the article a lot for me, but I'm not gonna go beat a dead horse.

Honestly the mayor's speech was more interesting to me than the first log. I'm still unclear on what was happening exactly with the ghosts and spectres. I did really like that one of them climbed a staircase the others couldn't see. When I was first reading the description, it felt very much like the skip was just a glitchy town, and while that's interesting in its own right, I am glad you added more. Between the ghosts, the mayor, and whatever happened with Adams, it might be going in too many directions at once, though. I, too, am colorblind and so that one particular log gave me some trouble as well.

Interested to see the changes made after the contest is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every time I read the title, I hear https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OdV_8TGswRA in my head.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
This article has had major edits made to it since the posting deadline, which is a violation of the contest conditions. Major edits are any edits you would not do to someone else's article, even if those changes are minor or adding in something small which was not present before. This includes adjusting the color of the article or adding in small details or tweaks. Please revert the major edits made to this article before the contest ends. If this is not done, the entry will be disqualified.

e: Article has been amended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's reasonable. How do the new colors work for you?

Tell me after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title was basically the inspiration for the whole piece. That's right, I said it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gee wiz, what a topical title.

In all seriousness, I dig it. The basic effect is simple, but interesting, and the speech from the mayor was a good read. I can see I am not alone in saying that the exploration log was a bit of a chore to read and comprehend, but once I got what was going on, each team member's experience was fun to compare and contrast to the other members of the crew. As Scorpion and Petrograd stated above this comes together as a pretty solid whole, and is certainly worth an upvote.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry to be nitpicky, but can something be done with red/green colorization? Confusing if you're colorblind, and although you can infer from the context it still breaks immersion when you have to guess like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This phenomenon is also apparent in recorded images and representations, which has complicated exploration and documentation. Subjects may perceive any visual or audio content related to SCP-3000-A uniquely, including photographs, videos, and computer-generated representations.

"This phenomenon is also apparent in" makes it sounds like recordings/representations are themselves affected, which is contradicted by the following sentence. Should be "This phenomenon also affects perception of"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the guy stealing candy.

This is great. A complete breakdown of consensus reality in a limited area, infecting those who stick around too long, orchestrated by some nefarious or possibly enlightened force, and there's literally no way to tell. Once I figured out the logs — and it only took me until the second one, really — I very much appreciated the way you present them. It illustrates the "everyone experiences this differently" in a way that's almost like experiencing it yourself. I strongly urge against changing them, as others have suggested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed both of those errors. I do intend to play around with the logs after the contest; I had to rush this piece out in order to get it in on time, so I apologize for the quality. It looks like the article will survive at least, which was my goal (I wasn't going to get above 200 in ten days regardless). Once this piece is resting comfortably in the new series, I'll give it a bit of a rewrite (including to that admittedly narmy line you pointed out, which I put in solely to give more context to the injury).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From what I can tell, they're mostly involved with other towns or urban locations that have weird spatial/extradimensional/occult weirdness going on, which was a neat touch.

As far as the overall article… +1. I'm largely in agreement with Scorpion451 here, it works as a whole.

Favorite moment: "Command says we're affected, evac is a no-go." Oh shiiiiiiiiiiiiiii
Least favorite moment:"Ghosts with guns.. a cruel joke."

Two things I noticed, at leas one of which can be edited I'm sure:
- Why doesn't Lincoln have a designation?
- You've got an extra "and" in the green text ("and and will die mercifully" if read right) in the speech.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm unfortunately short on time this morning and was unable to follow all of the links, but I'm curious- were the prior assignments of the mtf members in some way significant?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The logs were an interesting experiment, and I get what you were going for there, but I have to agree that they were extremely confusing.

I also think you might have missed an opportunity there: you could have included audio recorded from other nearby team members in each log. Even if we only got snippets of the other side of the conversations, it would make things much less confusing, and it would also let you play with variations between the conversations as their perceptions diverged.

Still, +1 because ultimately the article works as a whole, and the logs are a cool concept- just one in need of some tinkering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. And the logs were indeed confusing as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You almost lost me with those fractured logs, but I was able to piece it together. I still think that threading them back together would help with comprehension, and would still be able to convey the fractured nature of the situation, especially with some analytical commentary.

I'm going to +1 this in hopes it gets to the main list so you can play with those logs more and maybe come up with a better solution. Over all, I like it, though the title is a little misleadingly tongue-in-cheek.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry for only being able to provide piecemeal feedback, and not having time to clarify. I think the speech is a better addendum than the interview logs that you had previously - while the theme is a little lost (I suspect I only see it because I read the previous interviews), it is much more atmospheric. That's enough to get me to a neutral vote - still undecided over the mission logs.

I wonder whether re-ordering the tabs to Washington > Roosevelt > Madison > Lincoln > Jefferson > Adams might help with peoples' comprehension (or even swap Madison and Lincoln, although I assume you want the long ending of Lincoln to be in the second half of the logs)? That keeps a lot of the physical pairings together, making the story easier to follow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got mixed feedback on the log format but ultimately decided to keep it. I did this for a few reasons:

  1. Multiple members of the team split up. I found it easier to show those parts this way rather than synthesize the log into a normal format
  2. This way, the order in which you read the log is not chronological. So Adams has the last entry rather than Lincoln
  3. I wanted to emphasize how every person's experience in-A is unique

But now I've changed it to a format similar to the one you suggested, because overall I think it reads better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All surviving articles are going to be Series IV articles. The runners up through 5th place just get first pick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, right.

Well, after the voting is closed, maybe consider it.

(Runners-up are still considered for the mainlist, right? That was the case back in the 2000 contest.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IMHO the above suggested format change would really help your chances.

Too late for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh thank god. I was giving up on being able to actually read this.

I like the concept, but the execution of the mission-log was a deal-breaker for me. Meaning I didn't bother tabbing back and forth after literally every line, and mentally correlating timestamps, just to read it.

I get that the writer wants to emphasize the individual perspective of each MTF member, but what's wrong with plain old name tags? Current writeup emphasizes perspectives at the expense of continuity and reader immersion. IMHO the following format is more readable, and still makes it clear who is perceiving what:

00:00:00: Washington: All present. Let's move in.
00:24:06: Washington: Approaching the town.
00:37:20: Jefferson: Do you see that? Over there, by the lamppost. Looks like a ghost.

You know, like a movie or play script.

The only problem I can see with this approach is that seeing Jefferson: Jefferson during the check-in seems pretty redundant. The solution to this is to skip the first check-in.

Thanks again for the transcript, Dr. Coyle. Good luck in the contest, Rejekyll. IMHO the above suggested format change would really help your chances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anyone's interested, it was hard for me to follow the mission log, so I sorted it in Excel.

https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1_bLYYrwwQC3YA5fyaW2szwa4sej1-ZQXMA6Oyt9_yHQ/edit?usp=sharing
